event: Early Paleo-Indian hunter-gatherers settle the region, with sites such as Taima-Taima showing evidence of human presence and megafauna hunting.
Arawak-speaking and later Carib-speaking peoples migrate into the region, developing agriculture, pottery, and village settlements.
Indigenous chiefdoms such as the Timoto-Cuica in the Andes build terraced agriculture, irrigation, and stone-paved settlements.
Christopher Columbus reaches the Orinoco delta and Paria Peninsula on his third voyage, the first European to set foot on the South American mainland.
Alonso de Ojeda and Amerigo Vespucci explore the coast and Lake Maracaibo, naming the land 'Venezuela' (little Venice) after stilted indigenous dwellings.
Emperor Charles V grants the Welser banking family of Augsburg rights to colonize and exploit Venezuela, beginning German Klein-Venedig administration.
Spanish authorities revoke the Welser concession, returning Venezuela to direct Spanish Crown control.
Diego de Losada founds Santiago de León de Caracas, which becomes the colonial and later national capital.
The Spanish Crown establishes the Captaincy General of Venezuela, unifying the provinces under a single administration based in Caracas.
Francisco de Miranda launches a failed expedition to liberate Venezuela, raising an early version of the national flag.
Caracas patriots depose the Spanish captain general on April 19, establishing a governing junta and beginning the independence movement.
Venezuela declares independence from Spain on July 5, forming the First Republic.
A devastating earthquake strikes Caracas and the First Republic collapses as royalist forces under Domingo de Monteverde regain control.
Simón Bolívar leads the Admirable Campaign, retakes Caracas, and is proclaimed Liberator, establishing the Second Republic.
Royalist llanero cavalry under José Tomás Boves crush the Second Republic, forcing patriots into exile.
Bolívar's victory at the Battle of Boyacá leads to the creation of Gran Colombia, uniting Venezuela, New Granada, and later Ecuador.
The decisive Battle of Carabobo secures Venezuelan independence from Spain on June 24.
The naval Battle of Lake Maracaibo expels the last major Spanish forces, completing the liberation.
Venezuela secedes from Gran Colombia under José Antonio Páez, becoming an independent republic, the same year Bolívar dies.
Slavery is formally abolished in Venezuela under President José Gregorio Monagas.
The Federal War erupts between Conservatives and Liberals, a brutal civil conflict over land and federalism.
The Federal War ends with the Treaty of Coche and Liberal victory, reshaping the nation as the United States of Venezuela.
Antonio Guzmán Blanco seizes power, modernizing infrastructure, secularizing education, and ruling as a strongman for nearly two decades.
Cipriano Castro leads the Restoring Revolution from the Andes, beginning an era of Andean caudillo dominance.
Britain, Germany, and Italy blockade Venezuelan ports over unpaid debts in the Venezuela Crisis.
Juan Vicente Gómez seizes power, beginning a brutal 27-year dictatorship that lasts until his death in 1935.
Commercial oil discoveries near Lake Maracaibo begin transforming Venezuela into a major petroleum exporter.
The Barroso II well blowout near Cabimas confirms Venezuela's vast oil wealth, accelerating foreign investment.
Dictator Juan Vicente Gómez dies, prompting a gradual political opening under successor military presidents.
Venezuela joins the United Nations as a founding member, and a coup brings a reformist civilian-military junta to power.
Novelist Rómulo Gallegos is elected president in Venezuela's first fully free election.
A military coup overthrows Gallegos, ushering in a decade of authoritarian rule.
Marcos Pérez Jiménez rises to power and consolidates a repressive military dictatorship marked by lavish public works.
Pérez Jiménez is overthrown and the Punto Fijo Pact establishes a power-sharing democracy among major parties.
Venezuela co-founds the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) in Baghdad.
President Carlos Andrés Pérez nationalizes the oil industry, creating the state company Petróleos de Venezuela (PDVSA).
Currency devaluation on 'Black Friday' ends decades of stability and triggers prolonged economic crisis.
The Caracazo riots erupt against austerity measures, leaving hundreds or thousands dead in a violent state crackdown.
Lieutenant Colonel Hugo Chávez leads a failed coup attempt, gaining national prominence.
Hugo Chávez is elected president, launching the Bolivarian Revolution and a leftist transformation of the state.
A new constitution renames the country the Bolivarian Republic of Venezuela, while catastrophic Vargas mudslides kill tens of thousands.
A short-lived coup briefly removes Chávez from power before he is restored within days amid mass protests.
Chávez dies of cancer and Nicolás Maduro narrowly wins the presidency to succeed him.
Falling oil prices and economic mismanagement spark waves of mass anti-government protests.
Maduro convenes a controversial Constituent Assembly amid hyperinflation and intensifying political and humanitarian crisis.
Opposition leader Juan Guaidó declares himself interim president, sparking a contested dual-presidency standoff recognized by dozens of nations.
Maduro is declared winner of a disputed presidential election amid widespread fraud allegations and international condemnation.
